serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g) of prepared wholegrain cereal drink, typically made by dissolving the powder in hot or warm water.

nutrient_source.title: Wholegrain Cereal Drink (No Sugar) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Wholegrain Cereal Drink (No Sugar), cr_id D04010014, with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific wholegrain cereal drink (no sugar). GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: unsweetened wholegrain oat-based cereal beverage from the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 or below is classified as 'low'. Wholegrain oat-based beverages typically fall in the GI range 50–55. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 11.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 15.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (66 mg/100 g) and no added sugar
hypertension.guidance: With sodium at 66 mg per 100 g and no added sugar, this unsweetened wholegrain cereal drink is suitable for individuals managing hypertension; a 250 ml serving contributes approximately 165 mg sodium, well within the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (11.3 g/100 g) with low sugar (0.1 g/100 g) and wholegrain fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: The wholegrain base and dietary fibre (1.51 g/100 g) moderate the glycaemic response; however, a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 28 g of carbohydrate (GL ~15.6), so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair this drink with a protein source to further blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.47 g/100 g), low sodium, wholegrain ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low total fat, low sodium, and wholegrain composition of this drink are consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern; regular consumption of wholegrain foods is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, and the absence of added sugar avoids sugar-driven triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While this drink is unsweetened and low in sodium, it provides no significant anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, no polyphenols); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should complement it with omega-3-rich foods or anti-inflammatory ingredients such as turmeric or ginger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholegrain Cereal Drink (No Sugar) is a prepared beverage made from whole grains — typically a blend of oats, wheat, and barley — dissolved in hot or warm water. It is sold as a powder and reconstituted at home or in food service settings. The drink has a mild, lightly toasty, grain-forward flavour with a smooth, slightly thick texture. It contains no added sugar, with naturally occurring sugars at only 0.1 g per 100 g, making it a genuinely unsweetened variant. Per 100 g of prepared drink, it provides 60 kcal, 0.98 g protein, 1.47 g fat, 11.33 g carbohydrate, 1.51 g dietary fibre, and 66 mg sodium. Calcium (48 mg/100 g) and iron (0.35 mg/100 g) are also present. The wholegrain base contributes moderate dietary fibre and a low glycaemic index (estimated ~55, classified as low per University of Sydney GI scale), making it a more blood-glucose-friendly choice than sweetened cereal drinks. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its low sodium and low fat profile. For Type 2 Diabetes management, portion control is advised given the moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~28 g per 250 ml). This drink is popular in Singapore as a breakfast or morning beverage and is commonly found in hawker centres, coffee shops, and supermarkets. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal-friendly, and dairy-free, though it contains gluten from the grain base.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04010014) with a reported confidence of 0.95 and review status 'reviewed'. The source is a primary approved regional database for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific wholegrain cereal drink (no sugar) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D04010014) as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on unsweetened wholegrain oat-based cereal beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; GI 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney three-tier scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). All core macros (protein_g 0.98, fat_g 1.47, carbohydrate_g 11.33) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. Iteration 3 fix: corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55, which falls at the boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per University of Sydney classification.
